data.description:
The DM might also decide that certain environmental positions serve as a point of origin. A point of origin is typically a position in space, but some spells have an area whose effects extend straight from the point of origin to a location within the spell's area, with the target or effect included in the area if unblocked. Typical spells require you to pick one or more targets and use imaginary lines or cover to affect the magic. A spell's description tells whether the spell targets creatures, objects, points of origin, or other targets as explained in chapter 9.

CONE
Unless a spell has a perceptible effect, a creature doesn't know it was targeted by a spell until the spell is effected. For example, a creature doesn't notice a cone of cold until it starts feeling cold damage, unless the cone's width at its point of origin is greater than 10 feet or the cone otherwise creates obvious effects.

A cone's point of origin is where you are standing when you cast the spell. The cone extends in a direction you choose from that point of origin and has a specified angle of spread, given as an angle along its length. A cone's width at a given point along its length is equal to that point’s distance from the point of origin. A typical cone's maximum length is limited by its area of effect (see chapter 9). The point of origin is always included in the spell's area.
